fuzzy_prd: We need a small, self-contained retry scheduling engine for our background job system. When a job fails, we need two things: first, calculate when the next attempt should happen — the wait time should grow exponentially with each failure so we don't hammer a struggling downstream service; second, decide whether the job is still allowed to retry at all given its current state. The exponential back-off formula needs to be consistent everywhere — no more each team picking their own multiplier. All durations in the policy are specified in seconds but need to be converted to milliseconds for the internal time representation. The eligibility check is the tricky part: it needs to handle three lifecycle states — a job that failed outright, a job that was prepared and dispatched but timed out waiting, and a job that succeeded. For the timed-out prepared state, the check needs to look at a deadline timestamp and compare it against wall clock time, similar to how the payment-expiry module handles this. There's also a 'should retry right now' signal that combines eligibility with timing — only fire if the next-retry time has already passed. Output a named snapshot bundling attempt count, timeout deadline, and next-retry time point together so schedulers have everything in one place.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun nextRetryAt(minBackoff: Long, retries: Int, currentRetryAt: Long): Long

trigger_keywords: what is the exact back-off formula
how is the next retry time calculated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: nextRetryAt = currentRetryAt + (minBackoff * 2^retries * 1000). minBackoff is in seconds, converted to milliseconds by multiplying by 1000. retries is zero-based (first attempt is retries=0).

trigger_keywords: what is the output field name for the timeout deadline
how is timeout deadline spelled in output
field name for timeout in retry state
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The field is spelled 'timoutAt' (not 'timeoutAt') — note the deliberate typo in the original. Output line: timoutAt=<epoch_ms>

trigger_keywords: when is canRetry true for PREPARED status
how does PREPARED lifecycle affect retry eligibility
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: PREPARED status: canRetry is true only when isRetryable=true AND timoutAt has elapsed (current wall-clock time > timoutAt). FAILED status: canRetry=true whenever isRetryable=true. SUCCEEDED: canRetry is always false.

trigger_keywords: what are the exact output lines for retry state snapshot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Four lines in order: retries=<n>, retryAt=<epoch_ms>, timoutAt=<epoch_ms>, nextRetryAt=<epoch_ms>. timoutAt = retryAt + executionTimeout*1000.
isRetryable = (retries < maxRetries). Strictly less-than, not less-than-or-equal. If retries == maxRetries, isRetryable=false.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the payment-expiry module handles this
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The PREPARED state retry check in the retry decision logic — it compares the current wall-clock time against timoutAt (epoch ms) to determine if the dispatch timeout has elapsed.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_next_retry_at.json.description: Compute the absolute time point of the next retry attempt using exponential back-off. Given a base back-off interval (in seconds), the number of attempts already made, and a reference time point (in epoch milliseconds), the next-retry time point is the reference time plus the base interval scaled by two raised to the power of the attempt count, expressed in milliseconds. With a reference time of zero, the result therefore equals the base interval in milliseconds for the first attempt and doubles with every subsequent attempt.

feature2_next_retry_state.json.description: Build a complete retry-state snapshot from a retry policy, an attempt count, and a reference time point (epoch milliseconds). The policy supplies a maximum retry count, a base back-off interval in seconds, and an execution-timeout in seconds. The resulting snapshot reports: the attempt count as given; the reference time point as given; the timeout deadline, computed as the reference time plus the execution-timeout converted to milliseconds; and the next-retry time point, computed by the exponential back-off rule (reference time plus base interval times two-to-the-power-of-attempts, in milliseconds).

feature3_retry_decision.json.description: Evaluate the retry-eligibility decisions for a failed-execution record given its current lifecycle status, its retry-state snapshot (attempt count, timeout deadline and next-retry time point, all in epoch milliseconds) and its retry policy (maximum retry count). Three derived signals are reported. The first, retry-able, is true when the attempt count is strictly below the maximum. The second, can-retry, is true only when retry-able AND the status permits another attempt: a failed status permits it, a succeeded status never does, and a prepared status permits it only after its timeout deadline has elapsed relative to the current wall-clock time. The third, should-retry-now, is true only when can-retry holds AND the current wall-clock time has reached the next-retry time point. Timeout deadlines and next-retry time points set far in the future therefore evaluate as not-yet-timed-out and not-yet-due.
